summary: "Modifying published catalogs and tables in Qserv"
description: """The present implementation of the Replication/Ingest system's management system already supports deleting partially ingested catalogs (not tables) using the following resource:  ||method||resource||  |DELETE|/ingest/database/:name|    Unfortunately, the present mechanism leaves behind a number of other use cases which are still important. Hence the *main* objective of the proposed effort is to extend the existing implementation of the system's management interface (the REST API) to allow deleting select tables from the *non*-{{published}} catalogs as well as tables from the {{published}} catalogs, or the whole catalogs. These are examples of catalogs which may need to be deleted:   * test catalogs   * obsolete or wrong (based on the incorrect input) catalogs   * catalogs for which a new (better) version had been ingested   * less important catalogs (to clear disk space in Qserv workers before ingesting more important ones)   * catalogs contributed by users if a users is n longer interested in the catalog    The *second* objective of the effort is to implement a mechanism for *un-{{publishing}}* catalogs. In addition to the above stated scenarios, this one would also allow _extending_ existing catalogs with more tables, _chunks_, adding rows to existing _regular_ or _partitioned_ (_chunked_) tables, removing rows (based on a specific {{super-transaction}}) from existing _regular_ or _partitioned_ (_chunked_) tables. The proposed _bi-directional_ transitions of catalogs between the {{published}} and *non-*{{published}} states would be needed for   * QA-ing large catalogs during their _incremental_ ingests   * fixing problems with catalog ingests which were discovered during QA. This would be done by removing contributions into the catalog made during a specific {{super-transaction}}   * supporting user-level (contributed) catalogs which may be less static by their very nature compared with the production ones (LSST DRP, etc.)    Additional requirements/restrictions:   * all operations affecting *non-*{{published}} catalogs should begin with *un-*{{publishing}} a catalog first.   * the operations affecting the {{published}} catalogs will require adding extra security (management privileges) on top of what was already implemented with the {{auth_key}} based authorization: https://jira.lsstcorp.org/browse/DM-22745. One option here which won't require changing the protocol would be to discriminate different values of the keys to treat some of those as the """"high privilege"""" ones.   * the operation should *not* allow deleting the {{director}} table alone unless the whole database is being deleted.    Deleting a table requires eliminating the following data within Qserv and the Replication system's configuration:   * all instances of the table at workers   * an instance of the table from the {{information_schema}} table at Qserv {{czar}}'s database   * the the corresponding entry from {{CSS}}   * the table entry from the Replication System's Configuration    Deleting a whole catalog (database) requires deleting:   * all instances of the database at workers   * an instance of the database from the {{information_schema}} table at Qserv {{czar}}'s database   * the the corresponding entry from {{CSS}}   * the {{secondary_index}} for the {{director}} table of the database   * the {{empty chunk list}} file or the table.   * the database entry from the Replication System's Configuration    The proposed operation of *un*-{{publishing}} a catalog requires:   * eliminating the catalog from {{CSS}} to prevent Qserv from seeing the catalog   * reinstalling MySQL partitions (or creating the default partition *0* if none existed before) at all tables of the catalog. This step should also affect the {{secondary index}}    The the following resource are to be added:  ||method||resource||role||  |DELETE|/ingest/table/:name|deleting select tables of the *non*-{{published}} catalogs|  |PUT|/replication/database/:name|*un*-{{publish}} a catalog, so that it could be further modified|"""

summary: "Improve MultiProFit Parameter class performance"
description: """The MultiProFit Parameter class has some inefficiencies like entirely avoidable conditionals in the frequently called get_value and set_value functions; optimizing them could improve performance significantly (particularly for small footprints/galaxies where setup time is comparable or greater than actual model evaluation time)."""
comments: "The main problem was that I had a single Parameter class that supported transformed values with a boolean parameter in the get_value and set_value functions, necessitating avoidable conditionals. I created a new ParameterTransformed class and added get/set_value_transformed functions. This seems to have improved runtime by 10-20% in DC2 and COSMOS HSC, just because the get_value* functions get called so often and the overhead dominates over model evaluation time for a lot of very small footprints.    I'm not entirely happy with the solution and I'm sure it will need more refactoring in the future (particularly with regard to how it interacts with Limits), but I'm setting that aside for now. I suspect the best solution will be to move the relatively simple but frequently called classes and functions to C++ and Pybind them."

summary: "Extend Qserv Replication system to allow exporting tables/chunks"
description: """h1. Objectives  The proposed functionality of the Qserv Replication System would allow an efficient mechanism for:  * exporting (ready to be ingested) data for external users, LSST sites, or Qserv instances of the same site  * backing up catalog data  * _in-place_ re-partitioning and re-ingesting catalogs if a change in the partitioning configuration was required    This functionality would be especially useful (or be the only option) if the original (source) of data used for ingesting a catalog is either not-available, or it's difficult to use for (re-)ingesting the catalog.    h1. Implementation details  Here is a summary of changes/additions to the existing code:  * extending system's Configuration service to allow specifying an internal (at workers) _export_ directory where the *{{CSV}}*/*{{TSV}}*-formatted dumps of the tables/chunks would be temporarily stored before returned them to clients/requestors  * adding the _export_ service to be run at each Replication System's worker  * adding the client API (library) for interactions with the workers _export_ services  * adding a binary applications build upon the API for retrieving the data from the _export_ services and writing them as local files  * extending the REST API of the *Master Replication Controller* for retrieving connection parameters of the above mentioned _export_ services needed to retrieve a content of a _regular_ table or a _chunk_ (or _chunks_) of a _partitioned_ table  * unit and/or integration tests where it may seem feasible"""

description: """For schema stability we need a place where we can store identifying information for schema itself and various configuration-related info as well. That will server as a sort of metadata for the rest of the schema and data in registry. To be usable from many releases it itself needs to be super-stable but we also should be be able to store all sorts of metadata in it, exact structure of that is not possible predict today. I think the most generic solution for that is a simple key-value store with arbitrary keys and values encoded as strings. All other structures can be mapped to key/value using reasonable rules, though some care is certainly needed to avoid name collision."""

summary: "Have jointcal fail if the chi2 increases dramatically between steps"
description: """[~<USER> reported a [jointcal failure|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPXB4HG/p1590742434125100] where the fitter went from {{chi2/ndof~=40}} to {{~1e7}} during a single rejection step. That suggests something went catastrophically wrong with the fitter at that stage. I think the fit loop should probably exit in this case, with an appropriate error message, and not try to write output files.    I'm not sure what a good """"the fit got so much worse we should just stop"""" threshold should be, but probably {{delta-chi2/ndof>1e5}} is a reasonable start? I also haven't looked at the chi2 behavior during enough large data runs to understand whether significant increases in chi2 between runs is a common occurrence or not: that might be worth exploring.    A related question is what to do when the fitter does not converge after {{max_steps}}: currently jointcal logs an error but continues to attempt to write output. We should probably include a test for the chi2 value at the end of {{_iterate_fit}}, like was added in DM-15241 for a """"converged fit""""."""
comments: "I've learned some useful things as part of DM-25779, one of which is that chi2/ndof can increase because ndof decreased dramatically (e.g. due to changing what is being fit during initialization), while the raw chi2 decreases. This seems to happen somewhat frequently during photometric fitting for HSC, with increases of up to ~50% for one step not too uncommon). So an important question here is whether we should be checking against the raw or reduced chi2." summary: "Please make implicit Linearizer state changes explicit"
description: """Calling {{Linearizer.applyLinearity(detector)}} mutates the state of an “unpopulated” {{Linearizer}} so that it becomes specific to the detector that it was just handed.    This seems intrinsically surprising — why should calling {{applyLinearity}} change the state of my {{Linearizer}}?    It also leaves us open to bizarre situations like the Butler being used to fetch a linearizer for a particular detector, but the linearizer inadvertently being applied to a different detector... which then makes it _invalid_ for use with the detector it was fetched for.    My suggestion is that (as a minimum) {{Linearizer.applyLinearity}} should not call {{self.fromDetector}}; rather, all initialization that cannot be done in {{Linearizer.\_\_init\_\_}} should be done explicitly by the caller.    Stretch goal: when calling {{butler.get(""""linearizer"""", ccdnum=N)}} (or equivalent), the returned {{Linearizer}} should be already populated for the requested detector, and should not require the calling code to do any more work than simply {{apply}}‍ing it. (This is currently an issue in obs_subaru; I've not checked other cameras.)"""

summary: "Implement direct gradients in scarlet"
description: """Currently scarlet uses autograd to calculated the gradients used for optimization. This creates an overhead in both memory and runtime, as autograd requires a wrapper for all functions used in the code in order to back-propagate the gradients for each variable.    For scarlet we can calculate almost all of those gradients ourselves, eliminating the need to use autograd. The attached notebook demonstrates how this can be done, giving an outline of the runtime differences between autograd and direct gradients using a simplified modeling algorithm with a single back-propagation. It also demonstrates that gradients can be back-propagated up to 10 times faster per iteration when using gradients that we calculate ourselves, where the exact saving is based on the ratio of the PSF to the full size image (which translates into how much time we are spending on gradient calculation vs convolutions).    DM-23788 implements convolutions in real space, resulting in a model that allows us to calculate all of the gradients directly ourselves. However, it also demonstrates that real space convolutions are slower than those performed in k-space, so we are likely to use fft convolutions in production. We could in principle still use autograd to calculate the FFT convolution gradients for us, however since we only need the gradients for that single operation it will likely be easier to just implement a direct gradient for the FFTs as well (which may be as simple as calling a function in autograd)."""
comments: "I attached  [^direct_gradients.pdf] to give a (very rough) overview of the algorithm and where the expected runtime savings come from." [SEP] "Hi Peter,  Could you please review this ticket for direct gradients?    BTW: you are also the reviewer for DM-23788, which is still in review. Those changes are now included on this branch, so once we've finalized the review would mind also closing that one?    Thank you,  -Fred" [SEP] "A minor clarification. We don't get rid of autograd, we simply tell is how to compute gradients because we know most of these operations exactly. The reason for not giving up on autograd is that it allows us to include models for which we don't know the gradients analytically. It's a lot easier for the user to create a new source model this way.    Even if we give up on autograd, the concept of optimized custom gradient functions is supported by all autodiff implementations I know of." When issuing new tokens, it also seems to take quite some time before they start working.    This task tracks:    - Understand and document what the problem is.  - Add an alert for when Vault tokens are about to expire or have expired.  - Configure the TTL correctly and consistently and document how that is done.  - Investigate and fix issuance of new tokens.    DM-23487 isn't a strict dependency but probably should be done first. Ideally by the close of this story we should have a good tool set to automate inspection and issuance of Vault tokens and ideally storing them in 1Password."""

summary: "Add configuration protocol to DCS"
description: """Make sure that the DCS can send configuration parameters to the lower level components."""
comments: "I implemented a basic function to send configuration parameters to the lower level components. The function checks the configuration parameter values against the (hard coded) upper limits for those parameters. It also converts any quantity to radians if necessary since the lower level components expect radians while the configuration parameters are commanded in degrees. If a parameter value is too large or a parameter is unknown, a SAL AckError is thrown.    The function is written based on the assumption that the configuration parameters are provided in a dictionary form which directly can be passed on to the lower level components (apart from unit conversions). This assumption may not be true and will be checked at a later moment." [SEP] "Thanks for the review, [~<USER>."

summary: "Create a semantic validator for Felis-format TAP_SCHEMA data"
description: """Providers of tables to the LSP-facing database(s) and TAP service(s) need to be able to provide the input to the TAP_SCHEMA metadata required for successful service of these tables.    In the DM design this input is provided in [Felis|https://felis.lsst.io] form.    Currently it's possible to validate that Felis files are _syntactically_ correct, but not that they contain appropriate information for use as TAP_SCHEMA.    The task here is to provide a tool that reads Felis files and applies some rules to give feedback on the semantic validity and usefulness of the information provided, e.g.:   * Are table_index values present, non-zero, and unique within a schema?   * Have a subset of columns been designated as """"principal""""?   * Are the """"description"""" fields non-empty?    The messages could be characterized by severity levels depending on whether they are purely cosmetic issues or ones likely to affect functionality."""

summary: "Simplify simulation mode support in BaseCsc"
description: """At present supporting simulation modes in CSCs requires overriding 3 methods: BaseCsc.implement_simulation_mode, BaseCsc.add_arguments, and BaseCsc.add_kwargs_from_args. Simplify this as follows:    Add a valid_simulation_modes argument to BaseCsc. Default it to None for backwards compatibility. If not None then do the following:  * Test the {{simulation_mode}} argument in the {{BaseCsc}} constructor.  * Make {{BaseCsc.handle_simulation_mode}} a no-op.  * Add a {{--simulate}} argument to {{BaseCsc.amain}}'s argument parser, if there is more than one valid simulation mode.    This will greatly reduce the amount of extra work needed by CSC authors to add a simulation mode.    Also convert at least one other package to use this new technique, to check the design."""
comments: "The ts_ATDome pull request demonstrates that the new technique does reduce boilerplate for supporting simulation. It is almost entirely deletion. The down side is that CSCs that do not support simulation will have to add one line to avoid a new deprecation warning.    I also cleaned up some CSC shutdown code (make sure the start task is canceled when closing the CSC), after running into a weird issue while writing the new tests.    Pull requests:  * https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_salobj/pull/139  * https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_ATDome/pull/32" [SEP] "Reviewed in GitHub." [SEP] "Merged to develop.    I also filed DM-26531 to track packages using this support (and any others that need v6, but this change is likely to be the dominant factor)."

comments: "Summary on the proposed ideas:   * All steps are needed (if we build)   * Splitting the pipeline definition, we will not gain in speed   * Promote existing build, may require a full new implementation on top of what we have already    We could add some logic   * When we start the process, if the release candidate is exactly the same a weekly   ** This duplicate stuff in eups.lsst.codes, but should not be a problem   ** This requires to play a bit more with eups and see which possibilities it offers   * We could just do a git tag over the same tag   * But we need to generate src and binary pkgs, and this is what consumes time   * We could try to simplify this, seeing if it is possible just to do an eups copy of the existing package       * If the official release is then equal to the first release candidate we can do the same as above   * Or, we should build, exactly as we do now.   * All actual steps are required   * In this optic, we would introduce extra logic, that maybe not as beneficial as expected

summary: "Provide guidance in the developer guide about log levels, raise, and error handling"
description: """While exploring some HSC jointcal logs, I was reminded that we don't have guidance on how to handle """"not quite failure but possibly bad"""" situations. For example, an anomalously high chi2 at the end of a fit. Right now, for some of those cases jointcal emits a {{log.error}} message, because I felt that {{raise}}​ing an exception was not quite appropriate. In a private slack conversation on this topic, [~<USER> disagreed, suggesting that writing output and then raising was the correct approach. However, I'm not sure that that can work in the gen3 world, where Task output is fully decoupled from the algorithmic code.    I dug up [this 2 year old Community post|https://community.lsst.org/t/warnings-or-other-not-errors-in-the-stack/3452/8] on the same topic, where I also reference a related slack discussion. Our dev guide does not provide guidance on how to handle various error conditions, and we do not have any systems for analyzing logs to look for error messages beyond `grep` (which doesn't scale well to the amount of logs we produce, since someone has to do the grepping in person).    This ticket is for [~<USER> to come up with some such guidance. I don't know if that would result in an RFC, or if he should just write a section in the dev guide and we'll start using it."""
comments: "Note that we do have [guidance on logging levels|https://developer.lsst.io/stack/logging.html#log-levels], although it doesn't quite agree with the [Log4J definitions|https://musigma.blog/2017/11/06/logging.html] (in particular for {{warn}} and {{info}}).    I'm a little alarmed by the discussion cited in the issue description, since log-and-throw is generally considered an anti-pattern (the advice is that logging should be treated as a form of error handling). So I'd prefer an RFC for something this far-reaching, just to make sure we all agree on what should be done and why." [SEP] "{quote}  I was reminded that we don't have guidance on how to handle ""not quite failure but possibly bad"" situations. For example, an anomalously high chi2 at the end of a fit. Right now, for some of those cases jointcal emits a log.error message  {quote}  Not because I want to quibble, but because I think it's genuinely germane to understanding the issue: the situation described to me was “the code can fail and nobody notic

summary: "Allow non-saluser users to run jobs inside containers made from develop-env"
description: """The problem:    Jenkins runs under jenkins user. When starting a docker image, jenkins user (1003:1003) is hardcoded and passed to the container. There is a lot of workarounds, involving root user etc, all due to fact that /home/saluser is not readable to anybody else than saluser.    Possible solutions (in arbitrary order):   # add jenkins user/user with UID 1003 and give it some basic rights          2. /home directories are designed to hold user's content. What we see there is a project-wide infrastructure being kept in an arbitrary user home directory and used throughout the project. One obvious solution would be to run chmod a+rwX -R /home/saluser as root somewhere during building the container          3. probably better would be to abandon /home/saluser, and replace it for storage with e.g. /lsst, /vro or /opt/lsst or .. _(LBTO uses /lbto to keep project-wide stuff, and although that looks as a brutal attack to the Unix filesystem, it works - no need to store stuff important for us in /opt; probability some UNIX FS standard will in future use /lsst or any of the proposed directories is close to 0; if /lsst is controversial for storing dev repos, make it /lsst_repo, /lsst_dev/, /lsst/dev..)_ Give write rights to everybody in this develop dir, and make available command (with +s to root) to turn those into write protected directories. That way when building and installation of the software is finished, this script can be called, turn everything important into non-writable, making it impossible for users running container to modify its content (_any user can still run container with -u 0:0 if he/she/it would like to modify it's content)._    The fact that we will lose security for a while when building images is for me irrelevant - as noted above, anybody usually can run a container as root user as a workaround. As noted above, a script can be made to turn security back on for production images (and Jenkinsfiles can be configured to check for this after building Docker production Images..).    A note about directory: while /opt is used for third party packages, as noted above, this is proposed for LSST internal installations/containers, stored under lsstts org, and used primarily for LSST work. Once we have the installation process documented (in Dockerfiles building the containers), anybody can modify it to store packages as needed under /opt/mybestproject .. The distinction between /opt/lsst and /lsst is in this sense minimal, and /lsst will save a few bytes and keyboard hits while make the dir easier to notice and remember."""
comments: "I tried to chmod a+rwX -R /home/saluser, which fixed my problem I am encountering (attaching to image as -u 1003:1003):    [I have no name!@1759be2596e3 repos]$ source /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh   bash: /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh: Permission denied    after chmod:    [I have no name!@1759be2596e3 ~]$ source /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh   SAL development environment is configured         I am willing to do the changes as PR once we find consensual solution." [SEP] "A comment: repos are stored and then setup -r  to make them available in Python path. Having task that will install repo somewhere (/lsst/python, /lsst/lib/python) and have this in PYTHONPATH for container would be great. See for workarounds in e.g. ts_MTAOS Jenkinsfile, calling setup -r before running MTAOS tests." [SEP] "The path of ""/opt/lsst"" is used in DM docker image for the scientific pipeline stack." [SEP] "An additional argument for fixing this problem: Jenkins keeps branch workspace.

summary: "Investigate Faust for the kafka-aggregator app"
description: """As proposed in DMTN-082 the LSP users are generally interested in telemetry data at a frequency closer to the cadence of the observations.     The original proposal is that “all telemetry topics sampled with a frequency higher than 1Hz are (1) downsampled at 1Hz and (2) aggregated to 1Hz using general statistics like min, max, mean, median stdev”.     Commands and event topics should not be aggregated as they are typically low-frequency and can be read directly from the raw EFD data sources.    Another aspect of the aggregation is to resample the telemetry topics in a regular time grid to make it easier to correlate them.    The aggregator will produce a new set of aggregated telemetry topics in Kafka that can be consumed by a connector and stored in a specific format. In another ticket we'll use the parquet data format to store the aggregated topics.     In this ticket, we'll investigate and evaluate [Faust|https://faust.readthedocs.io/en/latest/], a Python asyncio stream processing library. Faust supports Avro serialization and multiple instances of a Faust worker can be started independently to distribute stream processing across nodes or CPU cores.    """
comments: "This ticket accounts for investigation work before adopting Faust. As a deliverable I would point to the aggregation example in the [kafka-aggregator repository|https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator].     See also the README in that repo for preliminary documentation about the use of Faust in this project."

summary: "Add system for pre-fetching, caching, and in-memory joins of dimension information"
description: """DM-24734 revealed that repeated lookups for the same dimension records make up a significant fraction of both Gen3 RC2 bootstrap (~47% in Oracle) and QuantumGraph generation (17% in SQLite).  This could be at least mostly addressed by caching more of those lookups, but we need to define interfaces that allow user code to make it clear when caches can be expired, and it would be even better to be able to do bulk fetches of these records to populate those caches up-front.    At the same time, discussions with [~<USER> about DM-21904 have revealed a very similar use case for pre-fetching and then making simple relationship queries (e.g. """"give me the tracts related to this visit"""") against an in-memory data structure.    I have an idea for how to deal with both of these together, and I think I just need to work out the interfaces in more detail with Nate to ensure it will meet his needs."""

summary: "Deprecate begin_<command> and end_<command> methods in BaseCsc"
description: """The begin_<state> and end_<state> methods turned out to be a bad idea. Raise deprecation errors when they are used, if possible (this may be a bit tricky, but we could """"call if found"""") and file a ticket for removal."""
comments: "Deprecating the begin_<command> methods turns out to be impractical because the basic do_<command> methods do useful checking that would be difficult to obtain if subclasses had to override these methods.    However, the end_<command> methods are less important, because the checking has been done by then. Still, if one overrides do_<command> to obtain end-of-state-transition behavior then it is crucial to call super().do_<command> first, or that checking will not be performed. To avoid the risk of getting this wrong it is safer to leave the end_<command> methods in place."

summary: "Create dataset class for processed bright star stamps"
description: """As part of bright star wings/extended PSF model (DM-23785), we will extract stamps around bright stars from calexps, apply some preprocessing, then save these (along with some metadata) to disk.    The reason this is needed is the code for DM-23785 will rely on coadding a very large number of small cut-outs around bright stars, from different calexps, together. These require some preprocessing steps, such as a normalization step, before being coadded. Since we cannot possibly store all stamps in memory at once, we cannot do this processing on the fly before coaddition, hence the choice to save them to disk.    Longer term, this choice will also enable us to change the way we build the extended PSF (aka bright star wing) models without having to redo the processing. As an example, we might (and likely will) come to realize we want a different extended PSF model for each CCD, or at least per some subregion of the full focal plane. If our initial run had all stars stacked together to build a single, constant model, we could reuse the processed stars saved at this stage to build the new models.    Regarding normalization of the stamps: while this will be carried out in the code for DM-23785, it is worth mentioning here. Since very bright stars are saturated, and otherwise contain various ghosts around their center, we cannot use usual (say, aperture) measurements of their flux. What we will do is instead integrate their flux in an annulus of constant radii around their center, far out enough that most ghosts are contained within the inner radius and do not impact this """"annularFlux"""" measurement. These values (one per star/stamp) are one of the things stored in the dataset introduced by this ticket. These only make sense in relation to one another if the annulus definitions used are the same; the class therefore contains checks that the same annulus radii (both inner and outer) are used, every time new stamps are added to the collection.       This ticket will add the class itself, and the associated info in obs packages.  """
comments: "Thanks for agreeing to review this, Nate!    As my first serious PR, I am afraid there is a chance this one will be ""not quick""."

summary: "Define and document ""address space"" for origin IDs"
description: """DM-21794 will split the dataset primary key into a compound key containing and autoincrement field and an """"origin"""" integer that represents _either_ (depending on the dataset type):  * the data repository or (in the future) data repository layer in which the dataset originated (e.g. DBB Chile vs. US endpoint, Bob's personal data repo layer vs. Alice's personal data repo layer in a multi-user multi-layer registry);  * for raws (and perhaps one or two other dataset types), an ID that corresponds to the instrument that produced the dataset, enabling a mangling of the exposure+detector IDs to be used instead of the autoincrement value, making the IDs for these datasets globally consistent.    To make this work, we need to decide how to assign those origin IDs, and that involves some careful thought about how to split up the address space (I'm assuming this will be another uint64, so we've got plenty of space) for both known entities, those we can partially predict (""""we will have per-user layers, but how many?"""") and those we can't predict at all (i.e. just reserve blocks of integers for later use).    We also need to decide how to manage that address space going forward:    - what is the source of truth (in the source code?  In a LDM document?)    - what is the procedure for using making use of address space we've reserved?   - how do we assign addresses to instruments/obs* packages?    I'm expecting the outcome of this ticket to be a document (DMTN or confluence).  If the proposal is to do a lot of it this in code or configuration that lives with code, prototyping that in daf_butler or another git repo would be welcome, even if it doesn't do anything yet."""
comments: "This has come up again in the context of Data Preview 0 in the sense that there is a possibility of the IDF and IN2P3 processing a subset of the DC2 data and we'd presumably like the raw data to share the same dataset_ids (invariant during ingest) for when we try to put the independent output runs back into a single combined registry. Or am I misunderstanding the point of this ticket.    For raw data isn't ""origin"" effectively the instrument name and the exposure_id the unique part that is invariant?  " [SEP] "I don't think this is hard at all for raw data alone. That is probably as simple as determining how to map each instrument name to an integer.    But if we want to use origin values to differentiate users in a multi-user system, we need to decide which origin values are reserved for instrument raws, and which ones are reserved for users, and that seems like something we should at least think a little bit about before assigning some origin values to instruments."

summary: "Change Datastore.getUri to Datastore.getURIs"
description: """When we started to allow datasets to be disassembled this resulted in a single dataset_id corresdponding to multiple disassembled files. This breaks the paradigm for getUri which assumes a single file.    On this ticket we propose to replace getUri with getURIs that will return a tuple of the URI of the primary dataset and a dict mapping component name to component URIs.    For normal disassembly the primary dataset would be None. In the future for virtual datasets this may no longer be true and the primary dataset could be defined with URIs to override components also being returned.    Predicted file will still assume no disassembly."""
comments: "[~<USER> there are the bulk of the changes in daf_butler but I also had to tweak ctrl_mpexec since it used the old API." [SEP] "Adding clarifying statements from [~<USER> on the dm-middleware channel:  getURIs returns two things:   * if it's not a composite you will get a single URI and a dict that has nothing in it   * if it's a composite you will either get a single URI *or* that will be None and you'll get a dict with Dict[str, ButlerURI]   * In the future for virtual composites you might get a primary URI *and* some overrides in the components dict" [SEP] "A couple comments/questions.   Good to merge."

summary: "Implement agent code generation"
description: """The general idea is that you can use a Faust agent to process messages from a given Kafka topic and add those messages to a Faust table for persistence. The table is configured as a tumbling window. The window configuration parameters are the window size and the expiration time. When the window expires a callback function is called to process the messages in that window. That's when the aggregation happens and when a new Kafka topic is produced with the aggregated values.    In a previous ticket DM-24600 we created Faust records dynamically which saved us from generating static code for the models in Faust.    After some investigation, we concluded that it would be difficult to create the agent's code at runtime.  We'll try the approach of generating the code for the agents from a template and use Faust auto-discover mechanism to import the corresponding modules."""
comments: "[PR #11 | https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/11] implements the Faust agent code generation using a Jinja2 template. Source topics to aggregate are discovered from Kafka based on a regex and a blacklist set in the configuration. This works for the aggregation example too.     The sequence of commands to run the Kafka aggregator looks like:    Initialize the example:         Generate the agents:        This command shows that Faust is able to auto-discover the new agent:         At this point Faust has everything it needs to run the workers, schemas for the source and aggregated topics are registered, Faust Record for the aggregated topic is created at runtime, etc.  Complete instructions on how to run the example can be found in the README." [SEP] "Hey [~<USER> I would appreciate your review here. Thank you!" [SEP] "Looks great. I put a comment in the PR about checking whether you need to add the template file to {{MANIFEST.in.}}" [SEP] "Thank you so much for the review [~<USER> I've implemen

summary: "Evaluate kafdrop UI"
description: """[kafdrop | https://github.com/obsidiandynamics/kafdrop] is an open-source web UI for kafka and can replace some of the functionalities of Control Center.  """
comments: "Some of the [kafdrop|https://github.com/obsidiandynamics/kafdrop] features:    * View Kafka brokers — topic and partition assignments, and controller status  * View topics — partition count, replication status, and custom configuration  * Browse messages — JSON, plain text and Avro encoding  * View consumer groups — per-partition parked offsets, combined and per-partition lag  * Create new topics    it has integration with the Schema Registry to deserialize Avro messages when displaying them.  I miss a couple of features compared to Control Center among them lack of support to Kafka Connect, ability to edit broker level or topic level properties like add partitions and change retention policy. Also the UI does not refresh you have to reload the app to update the data. But it seems a strong code-base and I would recommend it as a Kafka UI." summary: "Data Backbone Buffer Managers consistency checker"
description: """Need a program that can be executed on a subset of data to check that the Buffer Managers database entries match reality.  This work primarily is meant for the endpoint database, but there could also be consistency checks for the handoff side as well (for this ticket to determine).  This would include mechanisms to check the storage filesystem, butler repositories (focus on Gen3 repository as Gen2 should be going away soon), etc.  One would need to be able to provide a consistency checking plugin for every plugin that is being used on the endpoint side (e.g., when Rucio is added to the endpoint side, would need to sanity check DBB BM Rucio tables match Rucio state).  It is not the work of this ticket to make every consistency check.  It should include the framework such that others can write the consistency check plugins.  And to test the framework also code a storage filesystem check and a Gen3 butler check."""
